segments.speaker: 题目29/30/31/32
segments.text: Listen to a talk in a psychology class. Have you ever been so engrossed in a task that you completely missed something obvious? This is called inattentional blindness, a phenomenon where concentrating on one thing can make us overlook something happening right in front of us. A classic study illustrates this perfectly. Participants watched a video of people passing a basketball and were asked to count passes made by players in white shirts. Midway through the video, a person in a gorilla suit walked into the scene, thumped its chest, and left. Astonishingly, about half the viewers never saw the gorilla. Their attention was locked on counting passes. In real-world situations, inattentional blindness can have serious consequences. For example, while driving, focusing on finding a street sign might make you miss a pedestrian stepping into the road. Recognizing this tendency can help us stay safer and more aware. Interestingly, psychologists trace this phenomenon to a useful ability: selective attention. This is the brain's way of filtering sensory input to avoid overload, allowing us to concentrate on important tasks. Think about sitting in a noisy open-plan office writing a report. Phones are ringing, people are talking, but you manage to tune it all out and focus on your work. That's selective attention at its best. 听力Module 2
